CHARTS / BASKETS
% RETURN, REBASED · EOD 2026-06-15
Rotation ReadCitrini · claude

Tech is running, but the Mag 7 is absent from the move. AI infra averaged +24.3% and semis averaged +23.7% over the past month while the Mag 7 basket dropped -6.4% in that same window. XLK gained +5.99% this month and sits +28.07% year-to-date. ARM leads AI infra, MRVL leads semis. The gains are accruing to the infrastructure layer. Software is down -0.9% for the month. SaaS is down -2.5%. Capital spending is the trade.

The sector breadth supports the read. Materials gained +3.9% this month, real estate +3.68%, industrials +3.18%, financials +3.09%. Healthcare added +5.55% and is recovering from a -1.09% year-to-date hole. Energy is the clean loser: down -5.0% this month after a +26.07% year-to-date run. The commodity cycle trade is being sold. Communication services shed -4.56% as XLC/SPY entered the bottom 2% of its 1-year range. Consumer discretionary closed the month at +0.24%, still -1.48% year-to-date. Banks returned +10.7% on average as a theme, with C leading and JPM lagging.

The factor complex confirms this is broader than a handful of names. MTUM/SPY sits in the top 1% of its 1-year range. SPHB/SPY and VLUE/SPY are both in the top 1%. High beta, momentum, and value outperforming simultaneously means the rally has multiple leadership sources. The tell to watch is XLC: communication services at a z-score of -3.04 versus the S&P is the clearest marker that ad-revenue and consumer-facing growth is absent from this tape.

AI infra and semis average +24% monthly while Mag 7 drops -6.4%: infrastructure is the engine, software and mega-cap are giving ground

BULL

Factor breadth tells you this is real. MTUM, SPHB, and VLUE are all in the top 1% of their 1-year ranges. Five sectors gained +3% or more this month. Banks averaging +10.7% confirm the cyclical leg has substance.

BEAR

XLC sits in the bottom 2% of its 1-year range, XLY is flat at +0.24% for the month and -1.48% year-to-date, and the Mag 7 is down -6.4%. The consumer and ad-revenue layer has no bid. Fintech is down -25.5% year-to-date, uranium down -15.4%. The parts of the market pricing forward growth narratives are under sustained pressure.

TELL

XLC/SPY at z=-3.04: if communication services cannot stabilize here, the consumer growth story stays broken regardless of what ARM and MRVL do.

⚠ VLUE/SPY is in the top **1%** of its 1-year range (z=**2.68**, **+31.1%** year-to-date), signaling broad value outperformance vs XLF gained only **+3.09%** this month and is **-2.89%** year-to-date. XLY is flat and **-1.48%** year-to-date. The classic value sectors are absent from the leadership. — The VLUE stretch is being inflated by energy's **+26.07%** and materials' **+13.14%** year-to-date moves, not a rotation into cheap cyclicals. Trust the sector data over the factor ratio: do not position into XLF or XLY on the VLUE signal alone.
⚠ Banks as a theme averaged **+10.7%** this month, with C leading the basket vs XLF as a sector gained only **+3.09%** this month and remains **-2.89%** year-to-date — The sector ETF includes insurance, asset managers, and diversified financials that dilute the bank-stock move. Trust the theme basket over the sector ETF for positioning: individual bank stocks are running, but the sector allocation signal is weak. This is a stock-selection trade within financials.

The most stretched ratio is MTUM/SPY at z=3.8, in the top 1% of its 1-year range. That is a confirmed trend. You do not fade momentum at this reading without a defensive catalyst, and there is none visible today. SOXX/SMH at z=3.14 (top 1% of its range) shows the broader semi index outrunning its cap-weighted counterpart: breadth expansion within the semi complex, a constructive signal. XLK/SPY at z=2.94 (top 2%) and XLK/XLU at z=2.67 (top 3%) confirm the tech-versus-defensive spread is as wide as it has been in a year. EWT/SPY at z=2.73 (top 2%) with +45.9% year-to-date is Taiwan riding the semiconductor spending cycle directly. VLUE/SPY at z=2.68 (top 1% of its range) is likely being dragged higher by energy's +26.07% and materials' +13.14% year-to-date gains rather than a true broad value rotation. On the other side: XLC/SPY at z=-3.04 (bottom 2%) and XLY/SPY at z=-2.29 (bottom 4%) are statistically oversold on a relative basis, but neither has a catalyst to snap them back. BNO/USO at z=-2.29 (bottom 1% of its range) reflects crude curve pressure and aligns directly with energy's -5.0% monthly print. MCHI/SPY at z=-2.39 (bottom 4%) is a confirmed trend.

The 112 relative-strength ratios ranked by how far they sit from their 1-year mean. |z|≥2σ = extreme (mean-reversion candidate). Red = stretched high, green = stretched low. Click a row to open its theme.

IWM/SPY +8.5% +2.0σMDY/SPY +4.5% +1.0σRSP/SPY +1.1% -0.2σIWM/QQQ +0.1% -0.1σIWB/SPY -0.2% -1.1σQQQE/QQQ -0.6% -0.6σIWO/IWN -2.6% -0.5σMDY/IWM -3.6% -1.3σRSP/QQQ -6.7% -1.6σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line here is a ratio of two indexes by market cap; a rising line means the numerator is outperforming, signaling that market breadth is expanding to include smaller stocks. IWM/SPY leads the basket at +5.71% this month and +8.45% YTD, and its z-score of 1.99 sigma above its 1-year mean is the most stretched reading across all nine pairs, meaning small-cap outperformance is historically extended. MDY/IWM is the laggard at -0.74% on the month and -3.63% YTD, so mid-caps are quietly the weak link inside the size spectrum. At 1.99 sigma, this is a fading setup rather than a chasing setup; watch for either SPY to close the gap on IWM or IWM to pull back toward its mean.

VLUE/SPY +31.1% +2.7σSPHB/SPLV +19.5% +2.2σMTUM/SPY +18.2% +3.8σSPHB/SPY +15.1% +2.5σIVW/IVE +1.8% +0.9σSPYG/SPYV +1.8% +0.9σQUAL/SPY +0.3% +0.1σSIZE/SPY +0.0% -0.6σUSMV/SPY -5.5% -1.7σIWF/IWD -9.4% -1.6σMTUM/VLUE -9.8% -1.3σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each ratio shows a factor ETF priced against SPY; a rising line means the factor is beating the broad market, a falling line means it is giving back ground. VLUE/SPY leads at +10.94% this month and +31.12% YTD, one of the strongest value-over-market rotations you will see in a single year. IWF/IWD (growth vs. value) is the worst pair at -5.84% on the month and -9.43% YTD, confirming the rotation is deep and durable rather than a one-week noise event. MTUM/SPY is the most stretched at +3.8 sigma above its 1-year mean; momentum has run so far so fast that any macro reversal will hit that factor first and hardest. Fade growth bounces and watch IWF/IWD for signs it is bottoming before you call a growth comeback.

XLK/SPY +18.0% +2.9σXLE/SPY +16.1% +0.6σXLB/SPY +4.2% +0.1σXLRE/SPY +3.5% -0.5σXLI/SPY +2.7% +0.0σXLP/SPY +1.7% -0.8σXLU/SPY -5.0% -1.7σXLV/SPY -8.9% -0.9σXLY/SPY -9.3% -2.3σXLF/SPY -10.6% -1.4σXLC/SPY -12.0% -3.0σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket shows each sector ETF divided by SPY; a rising line means the sector is outpacing the broad market, a falling line means it is dragging. XLK/SPY leads at +5.54% this month and +17.95% YTD, keeping tech firmly in the driver's seat. XLE/SPY is the worst this month at -5.4%, even though XLE itself is up +16.11% YTD, meaning energy is shedding relative ground fast after a strong absolute run. XLC/SPY is the most stretched at -3.04 sigma below its 1-year mean, the sharpest underperformance reading in the basket. Communication services at that sigma is contrarian territory; any catalyst that shifts the advertising or streaming narrative could produce a sharp XLC/SPY mean-reversion trade.

XLK/XLU +24.2% +2.7σXLE/XLU +22.2% +1.5σXLK/XLP +15.9% +2.0σXLI/XLU +8.1% +2.0σXLB/XLP +2.4% +1.1σXLI/XLP +1.0% +1.1σXLY/XLU -4.5% -0.3σXLF/XLU -5.8% -0.1σXLY/XLP -10.8% -0.9σXLF/XLP -12.1% -0.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

These ratios pit cyclical sectors against their defensive counterparts; a rising line means risk appetite is expanding, a falling line means capital is rotating to safety. XLK/XLP leads at +6.09% this month and +15.93% YTD, with tech over staples as the cleanest expression of the current risk-on posture. XLE/XLU is the worst at -6.26% this month, cutting into its +22.25% YTD advantage and signaling that the energy trade is unwinding from a position of relative strength. XLK/XLU is the most stretched at +2.67 sigma above its 1-year mean; tech versus utilities at that extension is a late-cycle warning. You trim tech here or you wait for rates expectations to shift and utilities to close the gap.

TIP/IEF +1.8% +0.9σEMB/TLT +1.5% +1.7σHYG/IEF +1.1% +1.4σLQD/IEF +1.0% +1.8σMBB/TLT +0.7% +1.2σTLT/IEF +0.5% -0.4σSHY/TLT +0.5% +0.8σJNK/TLT +0.5% +0.9σAGG/TLT +0.4% +1.0σHYG/LQD +0.1% +0.5σBKLN/HYG -1.9% -1.3σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each ratio in this basket measures where money is flowing inside fixed income; a rising line means the numerator is outperforming, whether that is long-duration Treasuries gaining on intermediate paper or credit beating governments. TLT/IEF leads at +1.87% this month and +0.54% YTD, meaning the long end is gaining ground over the belly of the curve. SHY/TLT is the laggard at -2.61% on the month and +0.5% YTD, confirming that short paper is losing to duration as the market prices in a benign rate path. LQD/IEF is the most stretched at +1.8 sigma above its 1-year mean, a sign that investment-grade credit spreads are compressing against Treasuries. The data point that flips the entire basket is any SHY/TLT reversal back above flat.

QQQ/GLD +21.2% +1.4σQQQ/TLT +19.4% +2.4σSPY/GLD +11.9% +0.8σSPY/IEF +10.8% +2.1σSPY/TLT +10.2% +2.0σSPY/UUP +5.3% +1.6σTLT/GLD +1.5% -0.3σGLD/TLT -1.5% +0.2σHYG/SPY -8.7% -1.9σSPY/DBC -14.9% -0.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket measures how major asset classes perform relative to each other; a rising line means the numerator asset is winning capital flows from the denominator. TLT/GLD leads at +11.11% this month and +1.55% YTD, the sharpest single-month swing in the basket, meaning bonds pulled sharply ahead of gold in a move that reversed months of gold dominance. GLD/TLT is the mirror and the laggard at -10.0% this month and -1.52% YTD. QQQ/TLT is the most stretched at +2.38 sigma above its 1-year mean, meaning tech equities are priced richly against long bonds in historical terms. That compression in the equity risk premium tells you the market sees no rate risk ahead; a surprise bond bid or any rate shock would send QQQ/TLT back toward its mean and reprice growth equities alongside it.

USO/UNG +93.3% +1.4σUSO/GLD +87.4% +1.6σGLD/SLV +4.1% -0.8σGDXJ/GDX -1.3% -0.1σGDX/GLD -3.8% +0.1σSLV/GLD -4.0% +0.7σBNO/USO -6.9% -2.3σURA/SPY -9.0% -1.3σGDX/SPY -14.0% -0.5σPPLT/GLD -17.4% -0.3σPALL/GLD -19.1% -1.3σDBA/DBC -19.5% -1.5σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line here is one commodity ETF divided by another; a rising line means the first commodity is gaining ground against the second in relative terms, capturing momentum shifts between raw materials rather than absolute price direction. GLD/SLV leads at +5.42% this month and +4.11% YTD, meaning gold is outrunning silver and safe-haven demand inside metals is dominating speculative industrial demand. USO/UNG is the worst at -14.58% this month despite a massive +93.27% YTD, meaning natural gas crushed crude oil on the year but that edge is collapsing rapidly. BNO/USO is the most stretched at -2.29 sigma below its 1-year mean, meaning Brent crude is historically cheap relative to WTI. At -2.29 sigma, the Brent discount is the setup to watch; when global demand signals shift, BNO/USO mean-reverts sharply.

XLP/SPY +1.7% -0.8σXLU/SPY -5.0% -1.7σUUP/SPY -5.0% -1.5σUSMV/SPY -5.5% -1.7σGLD/UUP -5.9% -0.1σTLT/SPY -9.2% -2.0σGLD/SPY -10.6% -0.8σVIXY/SPY -14.9% -1.4σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

This basket shows safe-haven assets priced against SPY; a rising line means money is rotating to protection, a falling line means the risk-on trade is consuming defensive positioning. TLT/SPY is the leader this month at +2.22%, yet it remains down -9.23% YTD, meaning the year's risk rally has been so strong that the best single month for bonds barely registers against the damage. VIXY/SPY is the laggard at -11.81% this month and -14.91% YTD, confirming that holding volatility protection has been the most punishing trade in the basket. TLT/SPY is the most stretched at -1.96 sigma below its 1-year mean. You are not in a haven regime; you are priced for a soft landing, and any macro disruption that sends TLT/SPY back toward its mean will move fast and leave little time to hedge.

EWY/SPY +77.9% +2.2σEWT/SPY +45.9% +2.7σEEM/EFA +11.5% +2.1σEEM/SPY +11.2% +1.8σEWJ/SPY +5.0% +0.4σEFA/SPY -0.3% -0.4σVWO/VEA -4.2% -1.6σEWG/EFA -8.1% -1.6σEWZ/EEM -9.7% -1.9σMCHI/SPY -18.9% -2.4σFXI/EEM -26.6% -2.1σINDA/EEM -26.6% -1.8σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line plots a ratio: when it rises, the first ETF is outperforming the second. South Korea (EWY) is beating the S&P 500 by +11.66% this month and +77.91% year to date, the top performer in this basket by a wide margin. Taiwan (EWT/SPY) is the most stretched ratio at +2.73 standard deviations above its one-year mean, a reading that flags real reversion risk if the catalyst fades. Brazil (EWZ/EEM) sits in the bottom: -8.56% this month and -9.69% YTD. Asian tech exporters are doing all the work in international equity right now, not broad EM. If you want non-US exposure, check whether you hold Korea or Taiwan specifically, or you are sitting in a drag.

SMH/XLK +29.7% +2.1σKRE/XLF +15.9% +1.7σOIH/XLE +14.7% +1.5σSOXX/SMH +14.5% +3.1σKBE/XLF +12.7% +1.7σXBI/XLV +11.3% +1.1σXHB/XLY +4.5% +0.1σXRT/XLY +3.7% +1.8σIBB/XLV +2.1% +0.5σXOP/XLE +1.7% -0.4σITB/XLY +0.9% -0.6σXME/XLB -1.0% +0.6σIGV/XLK -31.0% -1.8σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line plots a sub-industry ETF relative to its parent SPDR sector. A rising line means the sub-industry is outperforming its bucket. Homebuilders (XHB) are beating consumer discretionary (XLY) by +11.33% this month and +4.45% YTD, a signal that rate-sensitive housing names are back in favor even as the broader consumer group stalls. Software (IGV) is losing to tech (XLK) by -7.85% in June and -30.99% YTD, one of the worst relative-performance stories in this basket across both timeframes. SOXX/SMH is the most stretched ratio at +3.14 standard deviations above its one-year mean: two semiconductor benchmarks rarely diverge this far, and you should watch that gap as a timing signal for the next rotation within semis.

ETHA/IBIT -24.7% -1.4σGBTC/GLD -27.5% -1.1σIBIT/SPY -34.8% -1.6σBITO/SPY -36.1% -1.3σIBIT/QQQ -39.9% -1.7σ
Rising line = first ticker outperforming the second (relative strength), rebased to 0%. zσ = how stretched the ratio is vs its 1-year mean (|z|≥2 = extreme).

Each line here is a ratio: when it rises, the numerator ETF is outperforming the denominator. Every ratio in this basket is negative this month. The relative leader, ETHA/IBIT, still fell -5.11% in June and -24.65% YTD, meaning Ethereum has underperformed Bitcoin on every timeframe shown. IBIT/QQQ is the laggard at -18.98% this month and -39.86% YTD, sitting at -1.73 standard deviations below its one-year average. Bitcoin is losing ground to large-cap tech faster than it is losing ground to Ethereum. You should not be using crypto as a Nasdaq-beta substitute right now.

XLK +28.1% +2.6σXLE +26.1% +1.3σXLB +13.1% +1.5σXLRE +12.3% +2.6σXLI +11.5% +1.7σXLP +10.5% +1.4σXLU +3.1% +0.4σXLV -1.1% +0.9σXLY -1.5% +0.0σXLF -2.9% +0.6σXLC -4.5% -0.5σ

This chart rebases all eleven SPDR sectors to zero so you can compare their return paths directly. Tech (XLK) leads at +5.99% this month and +28.07% year to date. Energy (XLE) is the June laggard at -5.0%, though it sits up +26.07% YTD, a split that shows energy dominated the first half and is now losing momentum fast. Communication services (XLC) dropped -4.56% in June. You are watching a rotation out of energy and communications, and into tech, materials, and rate-sensitive sectors. That is what the sector map says about where macro expectations are moving right now.

GOOGL +14.1% +1.2σNVDA +8.7% +1.2σAAPL +7.4% +1.3σAMZN +5.3% +0.5σTSLA -7.2% +0.2σMETA -12.8% -1.8σMSFT -17.4% -1.5σ

This chart rebases the seven largest US tech names to zero so you can compare their individual paths. The entire group is down in June: even the relative leader, TSLA, fell -0.87% this month and -7.22% year to date. AMZN is the worst performer at -9.93% on the month despite being up +5.32% YTD. When the leader posts -0.87% and the laggard drops -9.93% in a single month, the spread inside the basket tells you that position sizing within Mag 7 matters as much as owning the theme at all. You are watching a synchronized drawdown where dispersion is as important as direction.

MRVL +212.9% +3.8σMU +211.2% +2.9σAMD +128.9% +3.0σAMAT +111.0% +3.0σKLAC +99.2% +3.5σLRCX +98.2% +2.6σASML +60.1% +2.4σTSM +32.6% +1.9σAVGO +9.9% +0.9σNVDA +8.7% +1.2σSMCI -1.6% -0.8σ

This chart rebases semiconductor names to zero so you can compare their return paths. Marvell (MRVL) is up +65.57% this month and +212.9% year to date, almost certainly the result of a single earnings catalyst rather than broad sector momentum. Broadcom (AVGO) is the laggard at -9.18% on the month and +9.91% YTD. That spread between the top and bottom performer in a single month is extreme. When one name moves that far, the basket average misleads you: look at individual names for your entry, not the ETF price, and ask which companies have a confirmed AI silicon revenue event and which are waiting for one.

DDOG +71.9% +2.6σPANW +55.9% +3.0σCRWD +50.5% +2.8σNET +16.6% +1.2σMDB -14.2% +0.3σSNOW -21.3% -0.9σNOW -30.7% -1.2σCRM -34.6% -1.8σADBE -38.8% -2.1σZS -41.3% -1.5σTEAM -42.8% -1.0σHUBS -50.8% -1.5σ

This chart rebases software stocks to zero from a common start date. Cloudflare (NET) leads at +13.25% this month and +16.56% YTD, showing the market will pay for network infrastructure with durable revenue growth. Zscaler (ZS) is the worst performer at -25.86% in June and -41.28% YTD. The gap between NET and ZS tells you the market is not selling cybersecurity indiscriminately: it is distinguishing between the network layer and the endpoint or cloud-access layer. You own the winning sub-theme specifically, or you own the drag.

DDOG +71.9% +2.6σPANW +55.9% +3.0σCRWD +50.5% +2.8σNET +16.6% +1.2σMDB -14.2% +0.3σFDS -15.4% -0.7σSNOW -21.3% -0.9σPYPL -28.6% -1.5σNOW -30.7% -1.2σCRM -34.6% -1.8σWDAY -36.4% -1.3σADBE -38.8% -2.1σZS -41.3% -1.5σTEAM -42.8% -1.0σHUBS -50.8% -1.5σINTU -56.0% -2.0σ

This chart rebases 16 SaaS names to zero so you can see their full return dispersion. Cloudflare (NET) leads again at +13.25% this month and +16.56% YTD. Intuit (INTU) is the disaster: -31.36% in June and -56.04% year to date, a draw of that size in a name this large signals either an earnings implosion or AI displacement being priced in hard. With 16 names in this basket, the spread between leader and laggard is wide enough that an equal-weight SaaS position today is as much a bet on the laggards recovering as it is on the leaders continuing. Pick your names and know why you own each one.

ARM +231.9% +3.9σMRVL +212.9% +3.8σMU +211.2% +2.9σDELL +209.5% +3.6σAMD +128.9% +3.0σVRT +72.5% +1.4σTSM +32.6% +1.9σANET +22.2% +1.5σAVGO +9.9% +0.9σNVDA +8.7% +1.2σSMCI -1.6% -0.8σ

This chart tracks eleven AI infrastructure names rebased to zero, so a rising line means the group is collectively appreciating from a shared starting point. ARM has done almost all the work: up +77.02% this month and +231.92% year to date, while VRT fell -10.85% in June despite a +72.47% YTD cushion. A basket this concentrated in a single leader can produce a bullish-looking index line while most members go sideways or bleed. Before sizing up, check how many of the eleven names are actually participating in the move.

LMT +8.7% +0.3σGD +4.9% +1.2σLHX +1.1% +0.1σRTX -2.0% +0.4σBA -3.8% -0.0σNOC -6.0% -0.8σHII -14.9% -0.5σAXON -21.6% -1.1σPLTR -23.8% -1.6σ

Nine defense and security names rebased from a common date, so the line shows the group's aggregate direction. AXON leads this month with +10.61% but enters June -21.6% YTD, which makes the recent gain a partial recovery rather than a new leg. HII is the weakest name, down -9.62% this month and -14.89% YTD. The basket is splitting between tech-driven platforms recovering from a deep hole and traditional hardware names still in distribution. Watch whether AXON can close its year-to-date gap before the line can sustain a genuine uptrend.

CCJ +2.4% +0.3σDNN +1.0% +0.1σURA -1.2% -0.3σNXE -3.9% +0.1σUEC -15.9% -0.6σOKLO -26.1% -0.9σSMR -39.4% -1.2σLEU -40.3% -1.2σ

All eight uranium names are down this month, which means the basket line is declining from a recent high. OKLO is the least bad at -1.83% for the month but is -26.11% YTD. UEC is the hardest hit, down -16.44% this month and -15.87% YTD. Broad simultaneous selling across the whole group points to sector-level pressure, not single-name trouble. Wait for UEC (the closest thing to a pure-play miner here) to stabilize before treating any bounce in the basket line as a bottom signal.

AMGN +8.4% +1.1σLLY +4.9% +1.6σNVO -16.2% -0.8σVKTX -18.8% -0.1σ

Four GLP-1 names rebased to zero, and LLY is doing the heavy lifting. LLY gained +14.67% this month and now sits +4.87% YTD. NVO drifted -0.9% in June and is still -16.24% YTD. The basket line will largely track LLY's weight in it, so the chart is as much a read on one company as on a theme. NVO's persistent underperformance relative to its peer is a fundamental divergence: it is not a mean-reversion setup until there is a clear catalyst to close the gap.

IONQ +23.7% +0.9σQUBT -9.8% -0.7σRGTI -11.1% -0.1σQBTS -16.9% +0.2σARQQ -44.6% -1.2σ

Five quantum computing names rebased, with RGTI jumping +26.23% this month while still sitting -11.1% YTD. QUBT gained only +2.21% in June and is -9.81% YTD. There are no revenues anchoring these valuations, so the basket line is purely a sentiment gauge. A rising line tells you risk appetite for early-stage science bets is up. It is not a signal to build a position without a defined stop below a key level.

FTNT +87.9% +3.3σPANW +55.9% +3.0σCRWD +50.5% +2.8σOKTA +39.0% +2.6σNET +16.6% +1.2σS +1.4% -0.6σZS -41.3% -1.5σ

Seven cybersecurity names rebased, and the intra-basket gap is the widest in this set. OKTA gained +33.61% this month and is +39.04% YTD. ZS fell -25.86% in June and is -41.28% YTD. Those two numbers sit at opposite ends of the performance distribution inside a single sector. Stock selection matters more than sector weight here: an equal-weight basket line would average out a story that is, in practice, two completely separate situations.

XYZ +6.7% +0.2σAFRM -10.6% -0.1σHOOD -19.1% -0.4σNU -28.4% -1.5σPYPL -28.6% -1.5σCOIN -32.4% -1.4σSOFI -39.6% -1.2σBILL -52.2% -1.7σ

Eight fintech names, split cleanly between a retail brokerage recovery and a crypto-linked drawdown. HOOD gained +20.79% this month but enters June -19.11% YTD, so the move is a bounce from a depressed base, not a fresh breakout. COIN dropped -15.66% in June and is -32.45% YTD, the deepest hole in the basket. The basket line will flatten or fall as long as crypto-linked names drag against brokerage names recovering on activity tailwinds. Own the theme through the specific sub-story you actually want exposure to.

MRNA +61.7% +1.2σBIIB +12.5% +1.5σAMGN +8.4% +1.1σGILD +3.3% -0.0σVRTX -1.6% +0.2σREGN -21.1% -0.6σALNY -29.3% -1.4σ

Seven biotech and biopharma names rebased, with AMGN leading at +9.5% this month and +8.41% YTD. GILD slipped -3.15% in June but holds a +3.28% YTD gain. AMGN's monthly move roughly matches XLV's +5.55% sector gain, so large-cap biopharmaceuticals are tracking the healthcare recovery without breaking ahead of it. The question for the basket line is whether catalyst-driven mid-cap names can join the move or whether this stays a large-cap defensive story riding the sector bid.

GOLD +27.2% +0.0σAU +0.8% +0.3σFNV +0.4% -0.1σNEM -1.0% +0.3σWPM -1.5% -0.0σAEM -4.6% -0.3σKGC -9.6% -0.1σ

This chart rebases seven gold mining stocks to zero so each line shows cumulative gain or loss from the same start date, letting you compare individual names rather than the sector average. GOLD leads at +11.52% this month and +27.21% year-to-date; WPM has shed -10.34% in the same period and sits -1.5% for the year. Cost curves and balance-sheet quality are driving that +21.86-percentage-point spread between the two. Own the miners with the strongest margin structure when gold moves. Treat WPM as a mean-reversion candidate only if the spread stops widening and the metal holds its gains.

C +17.8% +2.3σMS +17.7% +2.4σGS +16.2% +2.3σKRE +12.5% +2.1σPNC +12.4% +2.0σUSB +9.3% +1.8σTFC +3.9% +1.4σBAC +0.1% +1.7σJPM -1.5% +1.5σWFC -12.0% +0.1σ

Ten bank stocks are rebased to zero here so you can compare return paths across the same earnings cycle and spot which franchises are capturing rate and credit tailwinds versus which are stalling. C is the standout, up +14.23% this month and +17.8% year-to-date; JPM is the laggard at +6.65% this month and -1.46% for the year. C's run reflects valuation catch-up and operational leverage to rate-sensitive revenue; JPM's drag tells you the premium franchise is already priced for execution and is compressing from there. Favor the cheaper, more operationally geared names inside financials. Watch JPM for a re-entry setup only when that valuation premium narrows toward the group.

MPC +59.6% +2.2σSLB +39.8% +1.6σPSX +37.4% +1.7σHAL +33.8% +1.4σOXY +33.4% +1.2σEOG +27.4% +1.3σCOP +21.0% +1.1σCVX +20.1% +1.1σXOM +19.9% +0.9σWMB +18.5% +1.1σ

This basket rebases ten energy stocks to zero inside a sector that is down -5.0% this month at the ETF level, so every line you see is a relative story about subsector positioning. MPC leads year-to-date at +59.61% while posting a modest +1.56% this month; XOM is the month's laggard at -8.4%, though it still holds +19.86% for the year. Refining margins have done the heavy lifting in 2026 and MPC's year-to-date premium reflects that. The pressure point: if crude keeps softening, crack-spread compression will catch up to that premium fast. Fade the integrateds on any bounce and stay with the refiner only while crack spreads hold.

CAT +52.2% +1.8σDE +23.7% +1.1σETN +19.6% +1.0σUNP +17.6% +2.0σROK +15.3% +2.1σHON +12.5% +0.2σEMR +5.3% +0.8σGE +4.5% +1.7σPH +1.1% +0.7σ

Nine industrial stocks are rebased to zero so you can see who is accelerating and who is giving back earlier gains inside a sector up +3.18% on the month. GE has surged +17.24% this month and now stands +4.54% year-to-date after a slow start; UNP is the laggard at -0.88% this month, holding +17.59% year-to-date from an earlier lead. The rotation is clear: aerospace and defense-adjacent names are taking over from freight and logistics. Hold GE for continuation. Watch UNP for a re-entry only when freight demand shows a hard catalyst, not just a stabilization in the chart.
